license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bp2ymqfw
title: High resolution negative silicone rubber impressions of perforation on stone personal elements for technological studies
author: Stefano Viola

steps_raw.instruction: Evaluation phase Careful evaluation of the degree of porosity and fragility of the object involved in the mold-making process. In our case it is a compact object, not cracked, made on a hard raw material (carnelian). It is very important to asses whether the surface may be stained by the use of low-viscosity precision dental impression silicones (by reading the scientific literature; carrying out preliminary tests on a small point of the object). The african carnelian bead (face A)

Preliminary cleaning phase of the archaeological object Gestures: actions prior to taking the actual mold impression. Not all actions are always necessary: for example, the last one (1.3) is mandatory in cases where we have to make impressions on an incomplete and/or unstable object.
Washing. Holding the piece in your hands, you clean it with demineralised water and then with acetone using a soft, clean toothbrush or cotton (about 3'). Water Acetone
Drying. Leave the piece to dry completely in an open resealable envelope to prevent the dust from settling again (time taken about 30', at a temperature of about 18° C).

Creation of support and object locking. In our case (broken bead with strongly biconical holes) it is necessary to create an adhesive paste support that blocks the piece and contains the silicone. A release agent layer is not applied because it will be on contact with a hard stone. The support made on adhesive paste

Pouring phase I Working time: 1’ Taking the molds by injection of impression material into the bead drill A and on surface of the object (3 set of drill hole impressions).Gestures: pouring by dispenser gun; ejection of a little ammount of mixed fluid (and discard it) from the dispenser to let air out of the tip; completely fill the end of the tip so that the inlet hole is full to prevent an air column from being injected (time taken: 30''). The complete Coltene microSystem ® Dispenser gun MKII; jet bite cartridge; mixing tip; oral tip

Drill hole impression A. In this case, strongly biconical holes, a impression is produced in two stages: first one face and then the other. The pouring starts from the bottom and continues with a circular movement to make the silicone adhere to the walls in the best possible way, continuing to rise in a regular way and remaining in an axial position to fill the voids. Face A, drill hole A

Surface impression A. Spread the silicone by injecting and advancing regularly to prevent air bubbles from forming. Leave the excesses of impression material to facilitate the subsequent demoulding phase. Face A, surface A
Hardening phase I The hardening (demoulding time of silicone : 2’) of the mixture begins as soon as you start injecting the mixture.
Demoulding phase I Gestures: To be carried out by hand or without sharp tools (plastic tweezers) according to a continuous and delicate movement. Face A

Pouring phase II Working time: 1’ Taking the molds by injection of impression material into the bead drill B and on surface of the object (3 set of drill hole impressions). Gestures: after changing the mixer tip and its terminal (oral tip), everything as phase 3, step 3.1 and 3.2 (time taken: 30'').
Drill hole impression B. Like step 3.1. Face B, drill hole B
Surface impression B. Like step 3.2. Face B, surface B
Hardening phase II Demoulding time of silicone : 2’
Demoulding phase II Gestures: like in phase 5
Observation of molds by microscope (checking for imperfections, air bubbles, etc.). Face A, sup A
Storage of molds in resealable plastic bags at room temperature. Molds in plastic bags

Final cleaning stage of the archaeological object Removal of any residues of silicone impression material. Any slight halo due to the silicone oil can be removed by washing the piece with distilled water and acetone.
Washing. Use of paintbrush, distilled water and dishwashing surfactant. Water and surfactant
Rinsing. Use of paintbrush and demineralised water. Water
Drying. Leave the piece to air dry completely (time taken about 15’, at a temperature of about 18° C). Air dry
